coredata.dc:title:
Peroxidase like Zn doped Prussian blue facilitates salinity tolerance in winter wheat through seed dressing 1

coredata.dc:description: Salt stress severely limits the growth and yield of wheat in saline-alkali soil. While nanozymes have shown promise in mitigating abiotic stress by scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS) in plants, their application in alleviating salt stress for wheat is still limited. This study synthesized a highly active nanozyme catalyst known as ZnPB (Zn-modified Prussian blue) to improve the yield and quality of wheat in saline soil. According to the Michaelis-Menten equation, ZnPB demonstrates exceptional peroxidase-like enzymatic activity, thereby mitigating oxidative damage caused by salt stress. Additionally, studies have shown that the ZnPB nanozyme is capable of regulating intracellular Na+ efflux and K+ retention in wheat, resulting in a decrease in proline and soluble protein levels while maintaining the integrity of macromolecules within the cell. Consequently, field experiments demonstrated that the ZnPB nanozyme increased winter wheat yield by 12.15 %, while also significantly enhancing its nutritional quality. This research offers a promising approach to improving the salinity tolerance of wheat, while also providing insights into its practical application.

The illustration of synthesis path of ZnPB

ZnPB could increase the wheat salinity tolerance by seed dressing and it could bring forth a high yield due to reducing osmotic stress and protecting macromolecules through alleviating ROS in plant cell.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Salt stress severely limits the growth and yield of wheat in saline-alkali soil. While nanozymes have shown promise in mitigating abiotic stress by scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS) in plants, their application in alleviating salt stress for wheat is still limited. This study synthesized a highly active nanozyme catalyst known as ZnPB (Zn-modified Prussian blue) to improve the yield and quality of wheat in saline soil. According to the Michaelis-Menten equation, ZnPB demonstrates exceptional peroxidase-like enzymatic activity, thereby mitigating oxidative damage caused by salt stress. Additionally, studies have shown that the ZnPB nanozyme is capable of regulating intracellular Na+ efflux and K+ retention in wheat, resulting in a decrease in proline and soluble protein levels while maintaining the integrity of macromolecules within the cell. Consequently, field experiments demonstrated that the ZnPB nanozyme increased winter wheat yield by 12.15 %, while also significantly enhancing its nutritional quality. This research offers a promising approach to improving the salinity tolerance of wheat, while also providing insights into its practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) stands as one of the most important cereal crops worldwide [1]. Salt stress stands as one of the most consequential abiotic factors jeopardizing wheat growth and crop yield. Exposing plant to salt stress typically leads to the excessive accumulation of cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS), including H2O2, O2 −, 1O2, and ·OH, thus significantly impairing the growth, development, and yield of food crops [2]. There is a pressing demand to mitigate salt stress and thereby enhance crop productivity and quality in saline-alkaline environments. Formerly, plant growth regulators like brassinolide have been implemented to ameliorate abiotic stress [3], leveraging the reduction of salt stress by scavenging excessive ROS in wheat [2,4]. In addition to plant growth regulators, diverse additives have been integrated into the soil, such as biochar [5], vermicompost [6], and plant hormones [7], aiming to minimize salt adsorption by wheat. However, the efficacy of current anti-salinity strategies remains limited and sometimes contentious, with most of the research still conducted in laboratory conditions [8].

In recent years, the interest in using nanoparticles as trace fertilizers has recently attracted attention because their small size holds the potential to perform endogenous functions within plants through entering its cells [9]. Among various nanomaterials, Prussian blue nanozyme, the first artificial metal-organic framework, obtained through the coprecipitation of FeIII salt and ferricyanide, has attracted researchers' attention due to its potential enzyme-like catalytic activity to attenuate ROS [10,11]. As reported by literature, Prussian blue still has shortcomings such as a relative weak catalytic activity and large size. Fortunately, through doping with different nonferric divalent species such as Zn2+ during synthesis, the catalytic property could be further improved due to the formation of metal nodes defects and modulated catalytic centers [12]. Moreover, it worth noting that no matter the application of Prussian blue or its analogues to increase the salt tolerance of wheat, particularly by investigating the catalytic activity of the nanozymes in relation to wheat's salt tolerance, has been rarely reported, and there is no mention of evaluating their effects in field experiments.

Therefore, in this study, Zn-modified Prussian blue (ZnPB) nanozymes were applied as seed dressing agents in field experiments to evaluate its effect in scavenging ROS as well as, attention, improving wheat growth in saline soil within real field environment. To elucidate the advantages of the application of Zn-doped Prussian blue, traditional ZnO nanoparticle regulator and carbon dots nanozyme as previously reported was chosen to be the comparison [13,14]. By evaluating the agronomic information and analyzing the plant physiology results, combined with detailed materials characterization, we demonstrate the mechanism of anti-salinity stress through the application of nano Zn-doped Prussian blue (referred to as ZnPB). We believe this report could shed light on the further investigation of suitable nanozyme to the improvement in the field of agriculture.

Potassium hexacyanoferrate (K3[Fe(CN)6]), zinc chloride, Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.0–38.0 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Poly (vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP K30) and 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. The assay kits for malondialdehyde (MDA), proline, total soluble sugar, total soluble protein, and α-amylase were purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute (Jiangsu, China). Unless otherwise specified, all reagents and solvents were used directly without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water.

The ZnPB was prepared by previous method with slight modification [15]. Briefly, 0.44 mol of PVP K30, 0.5 mol of Potassium ferricyanide and 0.25 mol of potassium chloride were dissolved in 500 mL of HCl (0.01 M) with intense stirring, followed by dripping 500 mL of zinc chloride (1 mol/L). After further stirring for 2 h, the white suspension was obtained by centrifugation. Followed by being washed with water three times to remove all residual precursor species, the obtained ZnPB was dried in oven at 80 °C and grounded, and finally stored for further tests. More details are illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The crystalline phase in the synthesized samples was measured by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Bruker D8 Advance instrument, with Cu Kα radiation: 40 KV, 200 MA, in 2θ range of 5–80°. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using an Agilent Cary 660 spectrometer. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope operating at a 200 kV accelerating voltage. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were carried out on an Axis Ultra DLD Kratos AXIS SUPRA instrument equipped with Al Kα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of peroxidase-like activity and steady-state kinetic assays
ce:section.ce:para:
The peroxidase-like activity and steady-state kinetic assays were measured according to the previous report with slight modification [11]. The peroxidase (POD)-like activity assays of ZnPB and carbon dots(CDs) were carried out at room temperature using 3,5,3′,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as substrates in the presence of H2O2 in 0.2 M HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 4). The absorbance of the color reaction was monitored in time scan mode at 652 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (TV-1, HTH instrument, China). Typically, reaction systems containing 6 μg/mL ZnPB solution, 30 % H2O2, 10 mg/mL TMB and pH 4 buffer solutions with the volume ratio of 1:3:1:20 were used to show the chromogenic reactions implying POD-like activity. The steady-state kinetic assays were conducted at room temperature in a 200 μL reaction buffer solution (0.2 M HAc-NaAc, pH 4.8) with 10 μL ZnPB/CDs solution (6 μg/mL) as catalyst in the presence of H2O2 and TMB. The kinetic assays of ZnPB/CDs with H2O2 as the substrate were performed by adding 10 μL of 10 mg/mL TMB and 10 μL different concentration (0, 3, 6, 12, 24, 48 mM) of H2O2 solution. All the reactions were monitored by measurement of absorbance at different reaction time. Catalytic parameters were determined by fitting the absorbance data to the Michaelis-Menten equation, which describes the relationship between the rates of substrate conversion by an enzyme (ZnPB in this study) and the concentration of the substrate, as shown in the following equation.
v =
v max
C
K m
+
C
In the equation, v is the rate of conversion, vmax is the maximum rate of conversion, [C] is the concentration of substrate, and Km is the Michaelis constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of nanozymes on germination attributes of wheat
ce:section.ce:para: Wheat seeds of Yangmai-25 were obtained from the Institute of Agricultural Sciences in Lixiahe Region in Jiangsu, Jiangsu Academy of Agricultural Sciences (JAAS). The seeds were sterilized with 0.6 wt% sodium hypochlorite solution and washed with distilled water prior usage. Then, solutions of ZnPBs and CDs (100 mg/L, 300 mg/L, and 600 mg/L, respectively) were mixed with the seeds at room temperature. The control samples were prepared by mixing the seeds with distilled water. All the treated seeds were air dried to their original weight and then placed on petri plates (30 seeds/petri plate) having double layers of filter paper [16]. The experiments were completely randomized design (CRD) with five replicates. For inducing salinity stress, NaCl solution with concentration of 6 wt‰ was applied. After 7 days, different parameters were measured and data was analyzed. Germination attributes including germination percentage, seedling length, and seedling vigor index were measured following the protocol reported before [17].

The field experiment was conducted in a farmland near the sea coast (33°20′00.39′′N; 120°73′64.71′′E) located in Yancheng, Jiangsu province, China. The pilot region is located in the northern subtropical monsoon climate zone, with an average annual temperature of 14.1 °C. The annual precipitation reaches to 1042.2 mm and the annual average relative humidity is about 80 %. The soil was a typical coastal saline-alkali soil, had high salinity and pH, low organic matter concentration, and was poor in available N, P, and K, with an average salinity of 0.2 %, and a pH of 8.95, and organic matter content of 8.978 g/kg. The concentration of available nitrogen of 0.805 g/kg, available potassium of 121 mg/kg, available phosphorus of 11.43 mg/kg. Soil texture was checked by using the process described by Bouyoucos [18], whereas, soil pH, by US Salinity Laboratory Staff (1954). Organic matter was checked through the procedure given by Walkley and Black (1934) [19].

The field experiment had 8 treatments along-with 4 replicates with randomized complete block design (RCBD). A uniform size of 25 m2 (5 m × 5 m) was selected for each experimental plot. 300 mg/L ZnPB and CDs were applied for seed dressing during plant growth, two extra different salinity resistant nanoparticles including Fe nano particles [20] and nano ZnO [21] were also applied as reference with the same concentration of 300 mg/L [8]. The selected levels of all four nanoparticles were based on the previous germination studies. The fertilizers were applied as NPK (where N for nitrogen, P for phosphorus and K for potassium) at a given rate of 40–60–20 kg/ha respectively which is normal practice of the local farmers. Full quantity of K and P while a half dose of N was added in the soil before seeds sowing, and rest half dose of N was added in the soil after 40 days of seeds sowing. Wheat (Yangmai-25), seeds were sown in each plot considering a seed rate of 225 kg/ha. The foliar spray was given during the elongation stage for one time, and control plants were sprayed with distilled water. Total volume used for each treatment for all replicates was four litters. Total four irrigations of raw-effluent were given to wheat until it ripened and uniform quantity of water was ensured for each replicate and treatment. At the ripening stage, wheat was harvested (after 18 weeks of sowing), and samples were separated into different parts for further analyses. Samples were oven-dried and stored in each relevant labelling for metal estimation.

At the harvesting stage, the growth attributes including plant height, steam diameter, and spike were recorded. The wheat grain yield for each of the treatment plot was also recorded. The biological yield of each treatment plot was also determined. All measurements were done in triplicate.

The anti-osmotic substances of proline, total soluble sugars in straw and grain, and soluble protein, as well as the lipid peroxidation substances contents of malondialdehyde (MDA) and total soluble protein in the harvest wheat straw were determined by the kit assay method from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute. All these results were finally measured with a microplate reader (Infinite M Nano, Tecan, USA) at the corresponding wavelengths.

Total nitrogen (TN) in straws was determined using an elemental analyzer (Vario macro cube, Elementar, Germany), total phosphate (TP) was assessed by molybdenum‑antimony colorimetry. To determine the sodium (Na+) and potassium ion (K+) in the plant, the harvest wheat straws were washed thrice using double distilled water and left for drying at 70 °C in an oven for three days. Next, 100 mg of oven dried straw were ground and digested in 500 mM HNO3 under 80 °C for 1 h. Then the mixture was digested in a fume hood for 12 h. The K+ and Na+ contents were determined with a BWB-XP flame photometer. The concentrations of K+ and Na+ were calibrated by using the standard curve of both ions.

The contents of starch, amylopectin, amylose, as well as α-amylase in the harvest wheat grain were determined by the kit assay method from Nanjing Jiancheng bioengineering institute. Different seed protein fractions were extracted based on the solubility criteria. The defatted wheat flour was sequentially extracted by the Osborne procedure with some modifications [1]. The albumin and globulin fractions were extracted collectively from the 0.5 g flour with 800 μL 0.5 M NaCl; the solution was intermittently vortexed and centrifuged for 5 min at 6000 rpm in a cooling centrifuge. The supernatant contains albumin–globulin. The pellet was washed, and then the gliadin fraction was extracted from the pellet by adding 70 % aqueous ethanol. The glutenin fraction was extracted from the pellet with 50 % 1-propanol +1 % dithiothreitol. The protein of each fraction was precipitated by adding an equal volume of acetone. The precipitate was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min in cooling conditions (4 °C). The pellet was dried overnight and solubilized in protein extraction buffer (Tris–HCl pH 6.8, 3 % SDS, 2 % glycerol, 0.7 % 2-mercaptoethanol). The concentration of protein in each fraction was estimated by Bradford assay [22]. The percentage composition of each fraction was calculated.

To determine the nutrient concentrations in grain, the concentration of nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), potassium (K) was measured with the same method described above. The concentration of calcium (Ca) in wheat straw was determined by plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (Nexion2000, PerkinElmer, USA), companying the samples digested with a mixture of HNO3 and HClO4 (4˸1, v˸v).

The data shown in the tables and figures are the means of three or more replicates and were analyzed using Microsoft Excel 2016 (Microsoft Inc., Washington, USA) and SPSS 22.0 (SPSS Inc., Illinois, USA). The figures were plotted using the Origin 2022 software (OriginLab, Massachusetts, USA). The mean values of the variables in each treatment were compared using Duncan's test at the 5 % significance level.

The Zn-doped Prussian analogue nanoparticles were synthesized based on our previous report [15]. The X-ray powder diffraction pattern indicates that the ZnPB possesses a crystal structure like Prussian blue without any detectable impurities (JCPDS no. 33-1061, shown in Fig. 1a) [23]. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy reveals two peaks at 2092 cm−1 and 1670 cm−1, which can be assigned to the -C ≡ N- and CO stretching of the PVP amide unit, respectively. Additionally, the peaks at 593 cm−1 and 486 cm−1 correspond to the stretching and bending of FeO bonds, while the peaks at 486 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of ZnO (Fig. 1b). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images demonstrate the formation of nanoparticles with an average dimension of approximately 25 nm (Fig. 1c), which is small enough to allow their penetration into testa and even enter wheat cells.

The chemical states of surface Fe, O, and Zn were investigated with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements (Fig. 1d-f). The high-resolution Zn 2p spectrum exhibited two distinct peaks at 1021.6 eV and 1044.5 eV, corresponding to the typical Zn 2p 3/2 and Zn 2p 1/2 states, respectively (Fig. 1d). Similarly, the high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum displayed two sharp peaks at 708.1 eV and 721.2 eV (Fig. 1e), which were identified as Fe 2p 3/2 and Fe 2p 1/2 of Fe2+. Additionally, two minor peaks at 716.0 eV and 734.9 eV were observed, corresponding to trivalent Fe centers [11]. The intensity of the Fe2+ peak accounted for 91.23 % of the total intensity, indicating that most of the iron in the ZnPB was present in its divalent form. This observation aligns with previous findings that the high molar ratio of Fe2+/Fe3+ promotes the predominant enzyme-like activity [24]. The O 1s spectra of the ZnPB were deconvoluted into peaks at 530 eV, 531 eV, and 533 eV, which could be attributed to lattice oxygen, chemisorbed oxygen, and adsorbed OH groups, respectively [25]. Furthermore, a peak was observed at 536 eV, most likely indicating the interaction of O2− with Fe and Zn in the ZnPB (Fig. 1f). This suggests that the central metal node in the ZnPB is predominantly in the divalent state, consistent with the Fe XPS results. For comparison, the characterization of CDs is presented in the supplementary information (Figs. S1-S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase mimetic activity of the nanozyme and the effect on ROS
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of POD can facilitate the detoxification of H2O2 within cells [26]. To demonstrate the POD-like catalytic activity of ZnPB, 3,5,3′,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was employed as the indicator. In the presence of ZnPB, TMB underwent oxidation within a 40-minute period, resulting in a deep blue color. This observation suggests that ZnPB can efficiently detoxify H2O2. In contrast, the control experiment and the CDs counterpart showed minimal change in color (Fig. 2a).

The steady-state kinetic behavior of ZnPB was measured and fitted using the Michaelis-Menten equation (Fig. 2b). As depicted in Fig. 2c, the Km values of ZnPB reached 4.409 mM, significantly higher than the CDs nanozyme previously reported (Km = 1.570 mM). This may be attributed to the high concentration of Fe2+ in the as-prepared ZnPB sample, leading to the active Fenton reaction of the Fe2+/H2O2 system, which transforms H2O2 into •OH [27]. Moreover, the dynamic redox changes in Prussian blue lead to the reduction of •OH and the highly efficient scavenging of ROS in the neutral cytosol [28]. Therefore, the POD-like activity of ZnPB can be described as follows:

TMBH2 and TMB represent the reduced and oxidized states of 3,5,3′,5′-tetramethylbenzidine, respectively. The high peroxidase (POD) activity of ZnPB presents the possibility for its application in mitigating stress in winter wheat growth under saline conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ZnPB improved winter wheat salt tolerance during germination
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we investigated whether ZnPB could alleviate salt stress in winter wheat through a germination experiment using a 6 ‰ NaCl aqueous solution on petri plates for 7 days. In this study, we used germination parameters such as final germination percentage (FG%) and phenotypical results of seedlings at 7 days after sowing to evaluate the effects of ZnPB seed dressing, as well as other comparison nanomaterials, including the widely reported agro-nanozyme carbon dots (CDs) [13,29]. The highest germination rate of 96.7 % was observed with the application of 300 mg/L ZnPB nanoparticles (Fig. 3a). This resulted in a 24.29 % increase compared to the untreated control check (CK) sample, which presented a germination rate of 77.78 %. In contrast, the application of nano Fe, nano ZnO, and CDs only led to a slight increase of 15.58 %, 10.00 %, and 18.56 %, respectively. Additionally, the application of ZnPB significantly increased seedling length, root length, and root dry weight among all these comparisons (Fig. 3b-e). However, except for the increase in root length (which showed a 13.95 % increase compared to CDs), the increases in phenotypic results by the application of ZnPB compared to other nanomaterials were not statistically significant. These findings suggest that the agronomic benefits are primarily attributed to the induction and facilitation of root growth by nanomaterials [26,30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ZnPB promotes winter wheat growth and yield in real life field environment
ce:section.ce:para: The practical effect of ZnPB on agriculture compared to traditional nanomaterials, especially nanozyme compartment CDs, was investigated in a real-life field experiment. Previous studies have reported a decrease in agronomic measurements of Control (CK) wheat plants grown in saline soil during the maturity stage [31]. Consequently, the variation in phenotypic attributes such as plant height, stem diameter, spike length, number of grains per spike, grain weight, and yield was investigated in the samples treated with nanomaterials and CK in saline soil after an 18-week duration. The study found that all four nanomaterial treatments showed increased salinity tolerance and plant growth compared to the control (Fig. 4 ). Comparing the plant height of wheat of CK and seed dressing with 300 mg/L Fe nanoparticle, a significant increase in height from 104.38 ± 3.49 cm to 118.30 ± 4.53 cm (13.35 %) was observed. Similar results were also observed in other treatments (Fig. 5a). However, when comparing treatments using different nanomaterials, the nanozyme groups showed the most significant effect on wheat height (CDs: 119.01 ± 2.29 cm, 14.02 %; ZnPB: 125.16 ± 2.36 cm, 19.98 %), with the highest increase observed in the ZnPB treatment. Similar results were observed for stem diameter and spike length, but, no significant increase was observed in the comparison involving treatments with nanozyme and non-nanozyme modifiers, particularly with the application of nano Fe (Fig. 5b and c).

It is worth noting that nanozyme treatment delivers significant advantages in terms of yield and grain properties. Fig. 5d-f shows a significant increase in grain number per panicle, thousand grain weight, and yield. Comparative analysis with wheat treated with Fe reveals that ZnPB can increase the grain number per panicle from 30.7 ± 3.93 to 33.1 ± 1.97 (7.82 %), thousand grain weight from 41.82 ± 3.88 g to 46.72 ± 1.01 g (11.72 %), and grain yield per 1000 m2 from 946.55 ± 43.58 kg/ha to 1061.61 ± 36.17 kg/ha (12.15 %). However, despite a slight increase in these indices through the application of the pre-reported nanozyme CDs, no obvious significant increase was observed in the anti-salinity experiment. Additionally, comparing ZnPB and CDs reveals a significant increase in grain number per panicle (7.82 %), grain weight (15.02 %), and grain yield per 1000 m2 (13.97 %). This can be explained by the relatively low peroxidase (POD)-like catalytic activity of CDs [32], which prevents efficient scavenging of ROS under salinity conditions [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ZnPB alleviate the cell membrane damage and osmolytes accumulation
ce:section.ce:para: The objective of this study was to evaluate the potential of ZnPB in reducing damage caused by salinity stress with investigating the malondialdehyde (MDA) concentration and the soluble protein concentration in harvested wheat straw. Winter wheats treated with hydro-dressing as the control showed significantly higher MDA concentrations, indicating cell damage and ROS formation under environmental stress [33]. Treating with only 300 mg/L nano Fe resulted in a decrease of MDA concentration in wheat straw, from 1.05 mmol/g to 0.44 mmol/g. Meanwhile, the application of ZnO showed a relatively delicate promotion in alleviating membrane lipid peroxidation, with the MDA concentration decreasing from 1.05 mmol/g to 0.96 mmol/g. Compared to these two non-nanozyme wheat growth regulators, nanozyme CDs demonstrated a better performance in reducing MDA concentration, with a decline from 1.05 mmol/g to 0.28 mmol/g. However, the most effective reduction in MDA concentration was observed in wheat straw treated with FePB nanoparticles, leading to a significant attenuation to only 0.19 mmol/g. This demonstrated a nearly 81.91 % decline compared to the control group and a 32.14 % decline compared to the CDs treatment (Fig. 6a). This result indicates that the exogenous application of ZnPB can effectively inhibit the membrane lipid peroxidation in wheat under salinity, even at a relatively low dose.

The accumulation of salinity ions can induce osmotic stress and disrupt ionic homeostasis within plant cells. A plant cell produces proline, total soluble sugar, and total soluble protein to mitigate osmotic stress caused by salinity ions. Essentially, increasing the levels of these substances can help restore homeostasis imbalance. However, a significant decrease in proline and soluble protein was observed after seed dressing. The concentration of proline in winter wheat dropped from 3.38 μmol·g−1 to only 0.90 μmol·g−1 with the application of ZnPB, representing a decline of approximately 73.37 % compared to the CK group. Meanwhile, the proline concentration in winter wheat with CDs decreased to 1.48 μmol·g−1, indicating a decline of only 56.21 % compared to the control group (Fig. 6b). Similar results were observed for total soluble protein (Fig. 6c). Plant growth under salinity stress depends largely on the ability to adjust turgor pressure using synthesized osmolytes to restrict the influx of Na+ [34]. The decrease in proline and soluble protein contents after treated with nanozyme can be attributed to the bolstering of plant immunity. This result demonstrates that nanozymes' ability to scavenge ROS contributes to mitigating salinity stress in wheat [14]. As a result, plant cells do not need to produce osmolytes to resist osmotic stress caused by salinity [35].

Interestingly, an unexpected increase in the effect of seed dressing on soluble sugar (Fig. 6d), including maltose and glucose, the important organic solutes for maintaining cell homeostasis, can be observed with the introduction of nanomaterials [36]. ZnPB treatment exhibited the highest increment, ranging from 4.83 mg/g to 15.03 mg/g, corresponding to a 211.18 % rise compared with only hydro-dressing wheat and a 21.01 % increase compared to CDs treatment. Soluble sugars, with their ROS scavenging properties [34], can accumulate more nutrients and enhance plant tolerance to various abiotic stresses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ZnPB regulate the K+/Na+ balance to attenuate osmolytes accumulation
ce:section.ce:para: Plant salinity tolerance is primarily determined by the ability to maintain ion homeostasis, specifically the balance of K+ and Na+ in plant cells [31]. K+ is an essential macronutrient that plays a crucial role in adjusting turgor pressure, regulating pH balance, and enzyme activity in plants [17]. It also promotes the water potential of wheat root vacuoles and helps maintain the flow of xylem sap in saline soil [37]. However, the accumulation of high concentrations of sodium ions leads to hypertension and reduces potassium levels, leaving a severe potassium deficiency in plant cells [34,38]. Therefore, this study measured the ionic content of straw, including Na+, K+, and the ratio of K+/Na+, at the maturity stage of wheat under various treatments (Fig. 7 ).

Na+ accumulated significantly in the straw during the maturity stage under saline conditions, reaching a concentration of 19.33 ± 2.14 mg·g−1. In contrast, all straws treated with nanomaterials exhibited a substantial drop in Na+ content compared to the CK in saline soil. ZnPB, as a seed-dressing regulator, resulted in the lowest increase of Na+ content observed, with a decline of 81.89 % (3.50 ± 0.36 vs 19.33 ± 2.14 mg·g−1 DW, Fig. 7a) compared to the control under salinity stress. However, after dressing with CDs, Na+ content decreased by 79.46 % (3.97 ± 0.09 vs 19.33 ± 2.14 mg·g−1 DW), with only a 10.26 % difference compared to the ZnPB treatment. This lack of a significant change suggests that POD-like nanozyme dressing could help reduce excessive Na+ accumulation and improve K+ retention in wheat under salt stress. The enhanced ability to exclude Na+ allows for the maintenance of a favorable cytosolic K+/Na+ ratio, thereby enhancing plant salt tolerance [39]. After calculation, the wheat sample treated with ZnPB exhibited the highest ratio of 6.43 ± 0.67 % among all treatments (showing 7.57 times more variation than the control and 71.62 % higher than the Fe treatment, while only 19.52 % higher than the CDs treatment, shown in Fig. 7b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced grain nutrition contents by ZnPB
ce:section.ce:para: The nutritional disparity resulting from salt stress is a significant factor contributing to the reduction in wheat yield [40]. The total NPK content in grains without seed dressing treatment exhibited a lower concentration compared to the treated comparisons. The NPK content in untreated CK grain is as follows: 1.72 ± 0.35 % for nitrogen, 2.15 ± 0.34 % for phosphorus, and 1.33 ± 0.12 % for potassium (Fig. 8 ). The exogenous application of nanomaterials, whether non-nanozymes or nanozymes, could significantly enhance NPK accumulation in wheat plant seeds. The nitrogen content in samples treated with nanomaterials exhibited an increase ranging from 1.80 ± 0.23 to 2.46 ± 0.42 % (Fig. 8a). The phosphorus content in samples treated with nanomaterials showed a rise ranging from 2.76 ± 0.23 to 3.34 ± 0.32 % (Fig. 8b). The potassium content in samples treated with nanomaterials displayed an increase ranging from 1.70 ± 0.13 to 1.98 ± 0.10 % (Fig. 8c). The highest uptake rate of NPK can be achieved in those treated with ZnPB, attains a content of 2.46 ± 0.42 % for nitrogen, 3.34 ± 0.32 % for phosphorus, and 1.98 ± 0.10 % for potassium, respectively.

As with the grains, there was no preferential partitioning of nitrogen in the straw. However, significant differences were observed in the concentration of phosphorus and potassium, like the results seen in the grain. This indicates the potential ability of seed dressing, particularly when treated with nanozyme ZnPB, to enhance the abundances of protein storage and synthesis machinery [41]. To verify this assumption, this study investigated the content of proteins, including the concentration of different protein fractions.

The total protein content of wheat endosperm consists of four fractions, namely albumin, globulin, gliadin, and glutenins. To quantify the different fractions of grain protein, the extraction process based on solubility criteria was utilized in this work. Upon seed dressing, there was a significant increase in the total grain protein content, increasing from 95.20 ± 11.94 mg/g of CK to 113.28 ± 15.94 mg/g, 113.65 ± 13.40 mg/g, 120 ± 16.31 mg/g, and 131.49 ± 9.94 mg/g after treating with Fe nanoparticles, nano ZnO, CDs, and ZnPB, respectively. The nanozyme-treated groups showed the optimal results, whereas the highest increment was observed in the ZnPB-treated sample, increased by 16.08 % compared to the control (Fig. 9a).

Regarding albumin and globulin, there was only a marginal increase in each treatment compared to the control sample. In the mature stage, the albumin levels increased from 16.26 ± 2.04 mg/g to 24.13 ± 4.28 mg/g, 25.10 ± 2.42 mg/g, 22.60 ± 8.75 mg/g, and 25.73 ± 2.66 mg/g after being treated with Fe nanoparticles, nano ZnO, CDs, and ZnPB, respectively. Meanwhile, the globulin content exhibited a relatively stable plateau state with slight, irregular fluctuations, ranging from 14.53 ± 2.50 mg/g in the control group to 14.44 ± 4.44 mg/g, 15.33 ± 4.66 mg/g, 16.89 ± 3.14 mg/g, and 13.58 ± 2.22 mg/g treating with Fe nanoparticles, nano ZnO, CDs, and ZnPB, respectively. It is worth noting that albumin and globulin are the initial fractions that accumulate in the developing embryo. As seed development progresses, the percentage of albumin–globulin decreases, while gliadin and glutenin increase [1]. This could explain the absence of significant changes in the levels of albumin and globulin. However, significant differences can be observed in the gliadins and glutenin levels. The gliadins in the control sample measure 9.32 ± 0.56 mg/g, while it increases to 16.61 ± 2.66 mg/g, 12.93 ± 2.10 mg/g, 14.74 ± 2.22 mg/g, and 17.94 ± 2.02 mg/g, respectively. The most significant result was found in gluten concentration under different treatments. The gluten concentration increased from 55.09 ± 6.84 mg/g in the control sample to 74.24 ± 3.04 mg/g (34.76 %) in the wheat treated with ZnPB resulting in a range of gliadins to glutenin ratios from 16.92 % in the control sample to 28.59 %, 21.45 %, 22.22 %, and 24.16 % after different treatment. The ratio of gliadins to glutenin is a factor attributed to the baking quality of wheat flour. Previous studies have shown that the composition of gliadins and glutens determines the functional and rheological properties of wheat dough [42]. A higher ratio of gliadins to gluten protein is associated with a higher ratio of wet gluten content [43]. Treatment with ZnPB resulted in a 32.87 % ratio of wet gluten content, 31.01 % higher than that of the control sample (Fig. S6). All the ranging trends of wet gluten content match well with the changing difference as the results in grain proteins.

It is interesting to discuss the increase in protein levels in wheat after seed dressing. Particularly noteworthy is the significant increase in protein levels observed in the nanozyme-treated groups. Therefore, ZnPB dressing demonstrated better protein yield in wheat, with priority given to the glutenin ratio. Moreover, free radicals, such as H2O2 induced by salinity stress, can bind to proteins and result in their destruction [41]. As ZnPB has been shown to function as a superior POD-like catalyst, it provides a greater opportunity to scavenge excess H2O2, thereby protecting proteins and enhancing their accumulation in grains [35].

However, a study conducted by Rapp et al. [44] revealed a negative correlation between grain yield and grain protein content (GPC) in wheat. This indicates that as the protein content increases, there is an inevitable decrease in starch. This research also investigated the starch content and its fractions. There was a significant decrease in starch content when seed dressing was added. The starch content ranged from 789.63 ± 26.93 mg/g in the control group to 716.48 ± 9.95 mg/g, 753.60 ± 40.5 mg/g, 707.35 ± 20.51 mg/g, and 700.67 ± 28.25 mg/g after different treatments. When examining the two starch fractions, amylose and amylopectin, a decline in content was observed in the amylose fraction when different nanomaterials were applied. Particularly, a significant drop was observed in the sample treated with nanozyme groups, with the highest decline found in the ZnPB sample (11.26 % decline compared to the control). Conversely, only marginal fluctuations were shown in the amylopectin content (Fig. 9b), suggesting a relatively insignificant increase in the amylopectin ratio within the total starch. Notably, the ZnPB sample had the highest amylopectin ratio of 52.84 ± 3.29 %, which was 11.55 % higher than the amylopectin ratio in the control sample of 47.37 ± 3.41 % (Fig. 9c).

Considering the decline of amylose and the unexpected rise of soluble sugar in straw, it reveals that this contradictory performance cannot be only attributed to the increment of protein. What's more, α-amylase is the predominant enzyme synthesized which mobilizes starch in plants, hydrolyzing the α-1,4-glucan linkages in the starch polymers to soluble sugars [36]. Hence, the α-amylase activity in wheats treated with different seed dressing was measured in Fig. 9d. Sample treated with nanomaterials presented a better activity of α-amylase in the grain with 2.05 ± 0.23 U/mgprot, 2.11 ± 0.36 U/mgprot, 3.31 ± 0.41 U/mgprot, and 4.50 ± 0.30 U/mgprot, after the application of Fe nanoparticles, nano ZnO, CDs, and ZnPB, respectively, higher than the control sample of 1.82 ± 0.08 U/mgprot. Besides, significant increment of α-amylase can be found in the nanozyme group, where the optimal result can be found in the sample treated with ZnPB, 1.47-fold to the control, and almost 42.86 % higher than the sample treated with CDs. This result could sketch a clear path of nanozyme ZnPB effect. Because of the accumulation of ROS in the wheat under salinity stress led to the degradation of enzymes due to peroxidation activity, accompanying with the increment of MDA and inactivation of α-amylase. The better POD-like activity of ZnPB scavenged the over-accumulated ROS, supporting the higher activity of α-amylase, further transferring amylose in the plant to more soluble sugar [36], providing more nutrients to supply wheat growth under salinity stress [8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-salinity stress mechanism based on the enhanced peroxidase like activity in ZnPB
ce:section.ce:para: Additionally, a significant increase in protein content can be observed in this process due to the protective effect resulting from the decline in ROS levels. The increased activity of α-amylase in the grain can facilitate the conversion of amylose into soluble sugars, thereby enriching the nutrient content in the presence of a saline stress environment [36]. Moreover, the relatively improved ratios of amylopectin and gliadin/gluten proteins achieved through ZnPB application contribute to the potential enhancement of wheat flour quality in the samples.

This work presents the preparation and application of a highly efficient nanozyme, ZnPB, like POD, for wheat cultivation under salinity stress. This study is the first to apply ZnPB with nano size (<30 nm) which allows it to easily penetrate plant cells and directly eliminate intracellular ROS. Versatile experiments demonstrated that ZnPB exhibits superior POD-like enzyme activity, surpassing the widely studied CDs. When used as a seed dressing agent, ZnPB resulted in a 12.15 % increase in wheat grain yield per 1000 m2, 13.97 % higher than the sample treated with CDs. Moreover, analysis of wheat physiological indicators revealed that the exogenous application of ZnPB, with its excellent POD-like activity, regulated intracellular Na+ efflux and K+ retention ability, resulting in a decrease in proline and soluble protein content in wheat. This alleviated the negative impact of ROS, thereby preserving macromolecules in cells and promoting an increase in the amylopectin ratio and the specific proteins necessary for bread baking. Consequently, the grain nutritional quality in typical saline soil improved. However, the safe security is still obscure due to the inefficient investigation of nanozyme materials, and more further works will be implemented in our following researches. In conclusion, despite some shadows still impeded in the future, the current research offers an alternative approach to enhancing both the quality and yield of wheat grown in saline soil and contributes to a deeper understanding of the practical application of nanozymes in agricultural sciences.
